aliases: Saʿid ibn Yusuf al-Fayyumi (سعيد بن يوسف الفيومي)
Rasag (רס"ג, acronym of Rabbenu Saadia Gaon)

roles: gaon (head of the academy of Sura)
summary: Saadia Gaon (Saadia ben Joseph al-Fayyumi, 882-942), born in the Fayyum in Egypt and appointed Gaon of the academy of Sura in Babylonia, is generally regarded as the first great systematic figure of medieval Judaism: philosopher, jurist, grammarian, liturgist, and translator of the Bible into Arabic (the Tafsir). His Book of Beliefs and Opinions (Kitab al-Amanat wa-l-Itiqadat, Hebrew Emunot ve-Deot) is the first systematic Jewish philosophy, drawing on the rational theology of the Kalam to argue the harmony of reason and revelation.
description:
Saadia ben Joseph al-Fayyumi, known in the tradition as Saadia Gaon (Rabbenu Saadia Gaon, the acronym Rasag), was born in the Fayyum in Upper Egypt and, after periods in the Land of Israel and in Baghdad, was appointed in 928 as Gaon, the head of the ancient talmudic academy of Sura in Babylonia (Iraq), one of the two central seats of rabbinic authority in the Geonic period. He is generally regarded in the scholarship as the first comprehensive systematic figure of medieval Judaism, active at once as a philosopher, a jurist (halakhist), a Hebrew grammarian and lexicographer, a liturgical poet (payetan), and a translator and exegete of the Bible. Robert Brody's study of the Geonim treats Saadia as the towering intellectual figure of that milieu, whose creativity ranged across nearly every field of Jewish learning of his day.

Several achievements stand at the centre of his legacy. His translation of the Hebrew Bible into Judeo-Arabic, the Tafsir, accompanied in parts by an Arabic commentary, made the scriptural text available in the spoken language of the Jews of the Islamic world and became the standard Arabic rendering for centuries. His Book of Beliefs and Opinions (in Arabic Kitab al-Amanat wa-l-Itiqadat, in the Hebrew translation of Judah ibn Tibbon Emunot ve-Deot) is generally described as the first systematic work of Jewish philosophy and theology: composed in Arabic and drawing on the methods and vocabulary of the Kalam, and in particular the rational theology of the Muslim Mutazila, it argues that rational inquiry, when rightly conducted, confirms rather than contradicts the received tradition of revelation, and it treats in turn the creation of the world, the unity of God, command and prohibition, the human being and the soul, and reward and punishment. In its introduction Saadia opens with a blessing of the God of Israel 'who is deserving of being considered the Clear Evident Truth' (Introduction, read on Sefaria), and sets out his purpose of helping believers whose faith is unexamined to reach the truth by rational demonstration. Sarah Stroumsa's studies place this project within the wider setting of Jewish and Islamic Kalam in the Mediterranean world, reading Saadia as a Jewish practitioner of the rational theology of his age.

Saadia was also a figure of controversy in the defense of the Rabbanite (rabbinic) tradition. He led the polemic against the Karaites, the movement that rejected the rabbinic Oral Torah and held that the law must be derived from scripture directly; his anti-Karaite writings (including works against the early Karaite Anan ben David) defended the authority of the rabbinic tradition and its chain of transmission. He was earlier drawn into the calendar controversy with Ben Meir, a Palestinian authority who sought to fix the calendar by the prerogative of the Land of Israel; Saadia supported the Babylonian reckoning. In his years as Gaon he came into a bitter dispute with the exilarch David ben Zakkai over the authority of the exilarchate, which led for a time to his removal and later reconciliation. These disputes are recorded here as historical controversies with parties named and not adjudicated; the anti-Karaite polemic in particular is presented as Saadia's own attributed position, and the Karaite view as an attributed position in its own right, the editor ruling on neither. He is presented on Judaism's own terms as the first great systematizer of the medieval tradition, with the philosophical and historical scholarship recorded alongside as attributed sources.
editorial_notes: This entry presents Saadia Gaon on Judaism's own terms as the first systematic figure of medieval Judaism, philosopher, jurist, grammarian, liturgist, and Bible translator (neutrality #1, #4, #6). His engagement with the Kalam and the Mutazila is stated factually as intellectual method and influence, not ranked against any other position (neutrality #10-#11). The controversies (the anti-Karaite polemic, the calendar dispute with Ben Meir, the conflict with the exilarch David ben Zakkai) are reported as historical disputes with parties named and NOT adjudicated (neutrality #1-#2, #7): Saadia's anti-Karaite polemic is given as HIS attributed position defending the Rabbanite Oral Torah, and the Karaite rejection of the Oral Torah is recorded as an attributed position in its own right, the editor ruling on neither. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-9: (a) academic historians of the Geonic period and of Saadia (Robert Brody, The Geonim of Babylonia, and his dedicated Sa'adyah Gaon monograph; Sarah Stroumsa on Saadya and Jewish Kalam); (b) the primary text, open and auditable on Sefaria (the Introduction to the Book of Beliefs and Opinions, read line-by-line, with Saadia's opening on truth and his stated purpose quoted verbatim); and (c) a contemporary Jewish scholar (Brody and Stroumsa both carry this lens; Daniel Frank on the Karaite counter-position). No encyclopedias (the Encyclopaedia Judaica, the Jewish Encyclopedia, Chabad.org, My Jewish Learning), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. Confidence: the Introduction to the Book of Beliefs and Opinions on Sefaria (Saadia For All / Kleiman Creative Commons translation) is rated high because it was read line-by-line for this entry, with the opening blessing on truth and Saadia's statement of purpose quoted verbatim; Brody's two books and Stroumsa's Kalam study are medium, verified via publisher and library records, not read line-by-line. FLAG: Sarah Stroumsa's 'Saadya Gaon: A Jewish Thinker in a Mediterranean Society' is a Hebrew study (Tel-Aviv, 2001), not an English standalone monograph; her English-language treatment is the chapter 'Saadya and Jewish Kalam' in The Cambridge Companion to Medieval Jewish Philosophy (2003), which is what is cited here (a book chapter in an academic volume, acceptable scholarship). Relation targets rabbinic-judaism, torah, oral-torah, tanakh, mishnah, talmud, and maimonides exist; emunot-ve-deot (the work) and karaites are dangling forward references to not-yet-created entities (flagged).

lifespan.note: 882-942 CE; born in Dilaz in the Fayyum district of Upper Egypt, appointed Gaon of the academy of Sura in Babylonia in 928, died in Babylonia. Dates follow the standard scholarship (Brody).

relations.note: As Gaon of Sura and defender of the Rabbanite tradition, Saadia stands at the head of the Geonic phase of rabbinic Judaism. The rabbinic-judaism entry exists.

Saadia composed the Book of Beliefs and Opinions (Kitab al-Amanat wa-l-Itiqadat, Hebrew Emunot ve-Deot), generally described as the first systematic Jewish philosophy. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created work entity (flagged); the kuzari and emunot-ve-deot works are authored elsewhere in this batch.

Saadia led the polemic against the Karaites (including works against Anan ben David), defending the Rabbanite Oral Torah against the Karaite rejection of it. Recorded as Saadia's attributed position; the editor does not adjudicate the dispute. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity (flagged).
Saadia's defense of the Rabbanite tradition turns on the authority of the Oral Torah and its chain of transmission, which the Karaites rejected. The oral-torah entry exists.
Saadia translated the Hebrew Bible (Tanakh) into Judeo-Arabic (the Tafsir) with commentary, the standard Arabic rendering for centuries. The tanakh entry exists.
His Tafsir rendered the Torah (the Pentateuch) into Arabic, and his defense of the Rabbanite tradition concerns the interpretation of the Torah. The torah entry exists.
As Gaon of the talmudic academy of Sura, Saadia stood within the Geonic tradition of talmudic authority and wrote halakhic works resting on the talmudic corpus. The talmud entry exists.
Saadia's halakhic and philosophical work builds on the rabbinic corpus of the Mishnah and its talmudic elaboration. The mishnah entry exists.

Saadia's systematic rational theology stands at the head of the tradition of medieval Jewish philosophy that Maimonides later brought to its height; stated as intellectual precedence in the tradition. The maimonides entry exists.

sources.citation: The Book of Beliefs and Opinions (HaEmunot veHaDeot), Introduction, open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria (Saadia For All: A Creative Commons Translation of the Introduction to Emunot Vdeot by Zachary Kleiman). Cited as the primary text for Saadia's opening blessing of 'the God of Israel who is deserving of being considered the Clear Evident Truth, Who makes the existence of the souls of speaking creatures absolutely clear to them' (Introduction 1). Read line-by-line and quoted verbatim from Sefaria.
sources.license: Hebrew original in the public domain; Sefaria's English translation (Saadia For All, Kleiman) under a Creative Commons license. Introduction sections 1-2 read line-by-line via the Sefaria text API.

sources.citation: Brody, Robert. The Geonim of Babylonia and the Shaping of Medieval Jewish Culture. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1998. The standard English survey of the Geonic period and its institutions, treating Saadia Gaon as the towering intellectual figure of that milieu, his conflicts (including with the exilarch David ben Zakkai) and the challenges from the Palestinian centre and from Karaism; cited for Saadia's life, his role as Gaon of Sura, and the Geonic setting. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Yale University Press record), not read line-by-line.

Brody, Robert. Sa'adyah Gaon. Translated by Betsy Rosenberg. The Littman Library of Jewish Civilization. Oxford and Portland: Littman Library of Jewish Civilization, 2013 (ISBN 9781906764906). A dedicated scholarly reassessment of Saadia's biography and his main areas of creativity (biblical translation and exegesis, philosophy, halakha, grammar, liturgy, and the anti-Karaite and calendar controversies); cited as the academic monograph specifically on Saadia. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Littman Library record), not read line-by-line.

Stroumsa, Sarah. 'Saadya and Jewish Kalam.' In The Cambridge Companion to Medieval Jewish Philosophy, edited by Daniel H. Frank and Oliver Leaman, 71-90. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2003. A scholarly chapter placing Saadia's Book of Beliefs and Opinions within the tradition of Jewish and Islamic Kalam and the influence of the Mutazila; cited for Saadia as a Jewish practitioner of rational theology and for the harmony-of-reason-and-revelation program. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Cambridge University Press record). NOTE: Stroumsa's 'Saadya Gaon: A Jewish Thinker in a Mediterranean Society' is a Hebrew study (Tel-Aviv, 2001); this English chapter is her accessible treatment and is cited in its place. Not read line-by-line.

Frank, Daniel. Search Scripture Well: Karaite Exegetes and the Origins of the Jewish Bible Commentary in the Islamic East. Etudes sur le judaisme medieval 29. Leiden and Boston: Brill, 2004. Academic monograph on the Karaite exegetes of the Islamic East who built a scriptural jurisprudence in opposition to the Rabbanite doctrine of a divinely given Oral Torah; cited for the Karaite counter-position that Saadia's polemic opposed, as an attributed position. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Brill record); carries the same medium rating it holds in oral-torah.json. Not read line-by-line.
